data.title: Eos Energy Enterprises: Gridscale Batteries Will Benefit From FERC Rule Making
data.description:
Good news has continued at EOS Energy Enterprises, Inc. (NASDAQ:EOSE) which has risen 87% from my earlier article "A Gridscale Battery In A Trillion Dollar Market" September 29, 2024. EOS manufactures a grid scale battery based on a zinc chemistry. These batteries participate in the LDES (long duration energy storage) market, with economic discharge times of 3–12 hours. The LDES market enables intermittent resources like wind and solar to be more available. LDES also allows shifting afternoon solar supply peaks to evening and morning demand peaks. This technology has significant advantages over lithium-ion in the same application:

  • Non-flammable
  • 97% domestic sourcing vs. Lithium, with cells mostly sourced in China
  • Insignificant capacity fades over 20 years
  • Able to perform multiple charge/discharge cycles per day
  • No need for active cooling makes it very quiet

Here's a list of company highlights since my September article:

  • The company hit its second set of milestones, allowing it to draw an additional $65 million on its facility with Cerberus
  • The company signed a deal for 216 MWh of energy storage with Springfield, Missouri
  • The company secured a 400MWh order for Marine Camp Pendleton
  • The company closed on a downsized loan guarantee of $303.5mm with the DOE. The first disbursement has been received.
  • The company drew its final $40.5mm on its main Cerberus facility
  • The company guided for 10x higher revenue in 2025

With other announcements strengthening the board and supply chain, the good news continues. I remain a strong buyer of the stock.

So, What Does The Future Hold?

An incredibly positive piece of news, for EOSE and the grid scale battery industry more generally, was released on February 12 about the PJM ISO: FERC approves PJM's fast-track power plant interconnection plan. We'll drill into that in a moment and go behind the news release to some better source documents. But first some background is helpful.

What's an ISO? What's PJM? The North American Market is divided into 9 different Federally regulated ISO's or Independent System Operators. Wikipedia on ISO's:

An ISO is an organization formed at the direction or recommendation of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC). In the areas where an ISO is established, it coordinates, controls and monitors the operation of the electrical power system, usually within a single US State, but sometimes encompassing multiple states.

and from the same source, here's a list of the 9 ISOs:

  • CAISO – California ISO
  • NYISO – New York ISO
  • Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT); also a Regional Reliability Council
  • Midcontinent Independent System Operator – Midcontinent ISO
  • ISO-NE – ISO New England
  • SPP – Southwest Power Pool
  • PJM – Pennsylvania-New Jersey-Maryland Interconnection
  • AESO – Alberta Electric System Operator
  • Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO); operates the Hydro One transmission grid for Ontario, Canada

And PJM, originally the Pennsylvania-New Jersey-Maryland Interconnection, is the largest of these, with the following stats:

serves 65 million people and has 185 gigawatts of generating capacity. With 1,436 electric power generators and 85,103 miles of transmission lines, PJM delivered 783 terawatt-hours of electricity in 2021.[5]

And here's a map of the area PJM serves:

A bit of recent history:

  • PJM has 287GW of projects waiting in their interconnection queue, or 155% of the installed capacity of 185GW listed above.
  • Average wait time for a project entering the queue now is estimated to be 2030
  • This is a market where the regulator, FERC, has been pushing the resistant PJM to queue reforms that have been successful in other ISO's.

So, What About That News Announcement?

Given the size of the PJM backlog in its interconnection queue and the length of time that backlog is taking, this news item has huge import for EOSE and other grid scale battery producers like Tesla (TSLA) and Fluence Energy (FLNC). One main positive in the news release is the following

PJM’s proposal removes most of the limitations it placed on SIS [Author's note: Surplus Interconnection Service] and allows requests for the service to be submitted earlier in the project development cycle. The SIS process runs separately from the interconnection queue process and takes less than half the time to complete.

The approved changes could unlock more than 26 GW of accredited capacity for the 2026/2027 delivery year [emphasis added]

Renewable Energy World defines SIS as follows:

Surplus interconnection service allows new sources of electricity supply to connect to the grid at the site of an already existing supply resource using the interconnection service already allotted to that resource.

In English: solar and wind don't produce all the time, so there is a "surplus" of unused time on the interconnection

Basically, this means this 26GW of capacity has been pulled forward from the 5-year average resolution, described above, to 1 to 2 years from now.

And What Is "Accredited Capacity" And What Does This Mean For EOS?

"Accredited Capacity" is an industry term which puts a dispatchability and reliability discount on actual physical installed nameplate capacity. ESIG explains:

Capacity accreditation establishes a way to measure the reliability contributions of individual resources to collectively meet the resource adequacy needs of the entire system.

And of course, one needs to look beyond the Utility Dive article's assertion of 26GW in "accredited capacity" to find out the actual nameplate capacity. And that is explained here:

One can see the Utility Dive article was referring to the 26.185GW in "Accredited Capacity" arising from 45.939GW in 4-hour battery applications. Since these are stated as "4-hr storage" this is 183.8 GWh of nameplate capacity and 104.7 GWh of "Accredited Capacity." Presumably, EOS has bids in this mass of BESS applications and will benefit from these applications being pulled forward.

But to me, a more interesting number is the sum of the nameplate ratings of Gas Turbine, Wind, and Solar equalling 154 GW. Each of those resources only runs part of the time, thus their much lower "Accredited Capacity." But each of those could be teamed with grid scale batteries such as those made by EOS and be utilized under this new "Surplus Interconnection Service" rule making.

EOS sells their grid scale batteries at about $250 per KWh, or $250,000 per MWh, or $250,000,000 per GWh. And to get to GWh from the 154 GW mentioned above, one needs to assume a duration of discharge. Let's assume 6 hours, which is above the 2–4 hours economically attainable by Lithium-ion but still a bit less than what is needed to shift a solar supply from an afternoon supply peak to a morning demand peak. This is 924GWh of potential grid scale battery demand, which has just been moved forward 3 years. And that is $231 billion at EOS's pricing.

What Portion Could EOS Get, And How Would They Pay For It?

Obviously, not all of this PJM total addressable market will be realized. Some of these applications will be withdrawn. Not all the gas turbine, wind, and solar installations which have an SIS component will get teamed with batteries. And clearly some of this will be scooped up by Lithium-ion incumbents like Tesla and Fluence (albeit as shorter, and non-optimal, discharges than the 6 hours assumed above). But let's assume EOS could be a player for 5% of that number. How does that rank in scale to existing operations, and how would they pay for it?

5% of the PJM TAM outlined above is 46.2GWh. EOS's sole automated line is expected to reach only 2GWh per year, so this would require 22 additional lines. And we know from the company's DOE loan press release that each 2GWh line must cost approximately $75mm:

[The DOE loan of $303mm]...is expected to fund the expansion of Eos’ manufacturing capacity to 8 GWh by 2027 to meet the growing demand for longer duration battery energy storage systems.

I actually expect the eventual line cost will be significantly less than this as the company gains experience, adds more automation capability internally, qualifies other automation suppliers, and generates more volume. But let's use $75mm per line for this exercise. And at that level, 22 additional production lines would cost $1.65 billion.

How could EOS shoulder this massive cost? As it happens, PJM has the highest concentration of data centers in the country. And we are facing a tsunami of new AI datacenters, that use 10x the power of the heavy demands of a normal datacenter.

PJM holds the largest concentration of data centers globally, with more than 4 GW located in northern Virginia alone, constituting >25% of national data center load.

As the data center operators build AI data centers, they are thirsty for additional power. One example of this thirst is Dominion Energy in Virginia, that is pursuing 19GW for datacenter projects-- demand they didn't have a year ago. Here are some of the links on energy investments for the players mentioned at the top of this article: Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL). Given that Small Modular Reactors don't yet exist, the regulatory process to reopen a shuttered nuclear reactor could take years, and Industrial Gas Turbines can take 2–6 years to commission the speed of battery systems that can be in place in less than a year must be attractive.

For the purposes of this exercise, let's assume that each of six data center operators advances $275mm to enable the construction of four automated lines. These lines might be 100% dedicated to their needs for the first year, 66% for the second year, and so forth. The $275mm capital commitment could be structured as $75mm of equity in EOS and $200mm of debt at a commercial rate, which would be 1/3 the rate of the expensive Cerberus financing. EOS gets a lower cost of capital, shareholdings from a gold-plated list of names, and the data center companies get clean, quick power for their main business.

Valuation and Risks

EOS has only just begun to generate revenues, will generate losses for 2025, and has not yet announced achieving the "contribution margin positive goal" they had set for themselves as of 4Q 2024. Yahoo puts their primary market cap at $1.08 billion, and fully diluted market cap is likely on the order of double that (depending on how many warrants get exercised). Stifel had estimated (@ August 2024) the 2025 revenues would be $325mm, so this is trading at a lofty 3x forward sales estimate.

The principal risk is execution and delay. The company announced a shortage of containers to house their product, during the 4Q of 2024. If such an event repeats, or if their automation does not continue to exhibit excellent results, shipments and revenues will falter.

Another risk often cited is the repeal or amendment of production credits under the Inflation Reduction Act. In that event, sales might be impacted. Some have worried about reversing DOE LPO loan guarantees. But since EOS has already drawn on this guaranteed facility, that seems unlikely.

Conclusion

I believe that examples like the new regulatory rule making for PJM underscore the need to think bigger and focus on acquiring the next 10 manufacturing lines and the next several sites.

PJM is one of the largest competitive power markets in the world. But reforms such as the one they are experiencing are happening in the rest of the nine ISO's in the US Market, and in other large markets on other continents.

EOS has yet to build, ship, and realize the revenue on their first GWh of battery. 2025 will change that. Our sights should be set considerably higher for later this year and the years ahead.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XP: Like A Bank But Conserves Optionality To Equities, It Is An Opportunity
data.description:
XP Inc. (NASDAQ:XP) reported Q4 2024 earnings, with growth in line with the broader Brazilian financial market. The company is closely resembling a bank operation, with more focus on advisory and financial planning as differentiator, because of the context.

XP trades at a P/E of 10x, which is high compared to bank peers like Banco Santander (BSBR), Itaú Unibanco Holding (ITUB) or Banco Bradesco (BBD). However, the company is much more levered to a potential recovery in Brazilian equities and a lowering of rates. For that reason, I believe the name is a Buy.

Q4 2024 and FY 2024 results

Banking over Brokerage: XP’s growth continues to be driven not by equity trading, but by banking activities. This is natural given the high interest rate (13% nominal, as much as 8% real) in Brazil, which does not show signs of receding in FY25.

Retail fixed income surged 49% YOY, coming from offering CD products to clients and trading in fixed-income securities. On the other hand, this was Corporate and Issuer services with 45% YOY growth, coming from warehousing (buying securities from companies and then reselling to retail) and wholesale banking.

Equities, on the other hand, were down for the year, something not strange in a year when the Brazilian ETF (EWZ) went down 30%, with high interest rates available with low-risk products.

Another sign of banking activity was cross-selling into classical banking products like credit cards (17% YOY TPV) or insurance (44% YOY premiums). The company launched consortiums, which are Brazilian saving plans for purchases like vehicles.

Good operational leverage: Operating leverage remains XP’s key margin driver. While revenues grew 15% YOY, SG&A expenses were held to 10% YOY growth, improving XP’s efficiency ratio to 34.7% (-157 bps YOY). XP plans to reach 30-34% EBIT margin in 2026, from 29% in FY24.

Competitive Strategy: Financial Planning

In a banking-prone context of high rates, it is difficult to compete with the large balance sheets of the main banks.

XP's strategy is to focus on its personalized advisory model as a differentiator. Management claims XP is the only financial institution in Brazil providing personalized advisory for clients with R$ 300 thousand (~$50 thousand) in assets.

The company is adding more internal advisors (the goal is to add about 800 per year from a basis of 3,000), and launched in Q4 a five-stage segmentation of clients: up to R$50 thousand only digital self-service, up to R$300 thousand with assistance in purchases and upsell of products, more than R$300 thousand financial planning, more than R$1 million financial advisory and above R$10 million wealth advisory and private banking.

Despite these efforts, results are mixed. XP posted R$20 billion in net retail inflows in Q4 (R$26 billion with corporate inflows). A good number but below the company's main competitor, BTG, with growth of R$30 billion for the quarter in its Q4 2024 quarter.

Valuation and Forward View

XP is increasingly behaving like a bank and with Brazil’s interest rates expected to remain above 10% (nominal) and 5% (inflation-adjusted) for at least another year, this situation is unlikely to change soon.

XP is not in the strongest position to compete in a bank-dominated market for the reasons we commented. In addition, a balance sheet business posses challenges to growth because it requires reinvestment of capital: with a 28% ROTE and a 70% payout ratio, XP can only grow its balance sheet by 9/10% per year without leveraging.

In this context, XP offers a P/E on TTM earnings of 10x, which seems high compared to those of the large Brazilian banks. I have recently covered Santander Brasil with a 14% earnings yield, and Bradesco with a 16% earnings yield, both growing at about the same rates as XP.

However, XP remains the best-positioned stock for a future decline in Brazilian interest rates and a bull market in equities. Whereas the banks have a bank-business, XP is half-bank and half-broker (or half-bank half-financial planner). If and when that shift happens, XP should outperform traditional banks.

This is why I believe XP is an interesting opportunity at a P/E of 10x while still growing structurally at 9/10% rates and potentially expanding margins by up to 5 percentage points (a one-time 20% growth jump). For this reason, I think the stock is a Buy.

data.title: Sohu: Cash Cushion Fails To Mask Ad Slump And Gaming Woes
data.description:
Sohu (NASDAQ:SOHU) reported Q4 2024 results, with brand ads and online gaming ahead of management expectations. Non-GAAP earnings were also ahead of expectations, driven by better-than-expected gaming performance and slightly lower opex.

However, management guidance for Q1 2025 is most disappointing. Brand advertising revenue is expected to decline at around 16% y/y at the midpoint of the guidance, and online games revenue is expected to decline at around 7% at the midpoint. Non-GAAP loss is expected to be $21mn at the midpoint.

We expect shares to be under pressure following this print, given that the weak brand advertising indicates not only the ongoing macro challenges that China’s ad environment is facing but also Sohu's relevancy and whether the platform can remain competitive in the new internet era.

As for gaining, the ongoing weakness continues to be disappointing as there is almost no material migration from the TLBB franchise, which is facing a combination of maturity and innovation challenges.

With the shares up 12% YTD, the market expects management to increase its shareholder return program. With $904mn in cash and short-term securities, Sohu is currently trading 47% below its cash value. We think management needs to optimize its cash reserves to either pursue incremental acquisitions and strategic and transformative business initiatives, or enhance shareholder return through buybacks and dividends.

We value Sohu at cash value, which implies a 100% upside from the current level. However, when compared against its peers, we believe there is an upside risk to valuation.

Assuming mid-single-digit revenue growth, Sohu could be valued at a similar multiple to its domestic peers, between 0.3x and 0.5x forward revenue. If revenue growth stabilizes, this would imply a 130% upside from the current level.

We recommend that investors who have not purchased this stock avoid it. For existing investors, we believe the downside risk is very limited given the significant discount to cash value.

Persistent Weakness In Branded Advertising Highlights Economic Challenges

Sohu’s branded advertising is facing a combination of macro and micro challenges. On the macro side, China’s stagnating economic growth is forcing businesses from Sohu’s key verticals in FMCG, internet services, auto, and properties to pull back on advertising. Although Q4’s 7% y/y decline is an improvement from the prior quarters that saw a 15-17% y/y decline in the prior quarters, the Q1 guidance of a 16% y/y decline in ad revenue underscores the persistent softness in China’s advertising market and the lack of meaningful near-term recovery.

While the company has attempted to drive engagement through unique marketing events, including collaborations with K-pop influencers and various offline brand activations, these efforts have not translated into sustainable advertising revenue growth, and this raises the question of whether Sohu’s ad portal can remain relevant as ad dollars are shifting to platforms with higher traffic and advertisers are becoming more sensitive with allocating ad dollars.

Weak Game IP Creation And TLBB’s Waning Relevance

Changyou’s TLBB franchise has been ongoing for almost 20 years since its founding in 2007. Changyou has been a key earnings driver for SOHU, but this unit has faced significant challenges over the past three quarters with downward momentum from the +24% y/y growth in Q2, followed by deceleration to high single-digit growth in Q3

The positive growth in Q2 and Q3 was driven by new in-game events tied to cultural festivals, but this effect proved unsustainable in Q4 when gaming revenue saw a 4% y/y decline as PC and mobile saw player churn despite skill set updates and new storylines.

We suspect the weak Q1 2025 guidance indicates further decline, which we believe will be driven by TLBB’s waning popularity, given its maturity and the shift towards Tencent’s Honor of Kings and PUBG Mobile titles over the years.

While the company has launched content updates and expansion packs to retain user engagement, these efforts have not been sufficient to offset the game's natural revenue decay. In addition, other titles such as NBA Absolute Superstar and Hero: Three Kingdoms have yet to offset the weakness in TLBB, implying that Changyou is facing innovation challenges similar to that of Tencent (see: Tencent: Geopolitical Risk Is No Game).

Strong Cash Reserves, But Capital Utilization Remains A Concern

We believe the only thing that is going for the stock remains its cash position and that any indication of upping share buyback or dividend could be a positive catalyst for the stock. The company ended the quarter with $904mn in cash and short-term deposits, implying the current valuation to be at a 47% discount to cash.

Under the current $150 million share repurchase program, SOHU only repurchased $52m as of February 13. The slow execution of the buyback program is due to trading volume limitations, as management aims to avoid excessive price fluctuations. However, the buyback program will take an extended period to complete at the current pace, offering little immediate relief to shareholders, in our view.

Given the company’s continued revenue declines, management must evaluate alternative capital deployment strategies such as transformative M&A or special dividends. Otherwise, revenue decline is likely to continue, and that share price could be under pressure in the foreseeable future.

Fair valuation At cash, With Upside If Revenue Sees Stabilization

Given the revenue decline, we believe SOHU should be valued at cash, which implies the share doubling from the current level.

If we assume revenue will stabilize and grow in the mid-single digits, its valuation could be on par with that of its domestic peers, such as IGG, which trades around 0.5x forward revenue.

If we were to assume a slight discount given the SOHU may have weaker revenue stability than IGG and apply a 0.3x forward revenue multiple, then SOHU could be valued at $34/share, or around 130% upside from the current level.

The risk to our thesis is that there is no dividend or any upsize of share buybacks. If this is the case, then we could see shares give up most of the gains YTD, although we do feel that the downside risk is limited given the wide discount to cash the stock is currently trading at. Simply put, very little downside risk yet very limited upside risk.

As an alternative, we believe investors should look to Chinese internet companies with global growth potential. Our preferred picks are PDD (see: PDD: Navigating Tariff Uncertainty, Driving Long-Term Growth Through Global Expansion) and Meituan (see: Meituan: Capturing The Lens Of Opportunity).

data.title: Ituran Q4 Earnings Preview: More Room To Grow
data.description:
I am initiating coverage on Ituran Location and Control Ltd. (NASDAQ:ITRN) in advance of Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings, which will be released on February 26th.

Ituran Location and Control Ltd. is an Israeli company that provides stolen vehicle recovery and tracking services along with a “full suite” of services for the connected car. The company distributes via original equipment manufacturers, after-market suppliers, financing companies, and insurance companies.

Ituran was stagnant for much of the last year before starting a run-up in November that has resulted in a total one-year growth of 59%.

Despite the recent run-up in share price, I believe that Ituran has more room to grow. Earnings are more than likely to be strong and on track with guidance. In addition, industry tailwinds for connected car technology continue to accelerate.

At a DCF-generated price target of $50.85, 19% upside from today's prices, I rate Ituran Location and Control Ltd. a buy.

Q4 And Full Year 2024 Earnings Preview

Ituran will announce earnings during the market on February 26th. They are expected to announce EPS of $0.68 and revenue of $81.8 million, both of which would be down sequentially.

Ituran has had significant beats in 6 of the last 7 quarters, suggesting guidance and consensus are leaning on the conservative side. Based on this trend as well as year-to-date performance, I am expecting earnings to come in at or above consensus, and likely up sequentially.

Consensus does keep Ituran on track for guidance. The organization has guided EBITDA of $90-95 million for 2024 and over $100 million for 2025.

As earnings are released I will be laser-focused on subscriber growth, especially any discussion around newer markets like India which could significantly impact growth expectations.

Valuation

To set a price target, I ran a DCF analysis on Ituran using the following assumptions:

  • A discount rate of 9% is based on the estimated WACC.
  • Near-term revenue growth of 4% (well below the industry but in line with guidance) and SG&A growth of 2% are in line with estimated inflation as the costs have been scaling nicely.
  • Long-run growth rate of 2% maintaining current growth trajectory into the future.

This DCF analysis yields a price target of $50.85, a 19% upside from today's pricing.

Wall Street analysts are less optimistic at a price target of $36.85. I do want to note that this price target was set before the recent run-up, so this may not be considered the best available information.

The quant rating is signaling a strong buy with above-average ratings across all metrics except growth, which receives a C+. Overall, the quant is in alignment with my thesis, as my projection does not require outsize growth or even keep up with it.

Ituran Can Ride Easy On Tailwinds

The connected car market is a great place to be sitting with accelerating tailwinds. The great news for Ituran is that, by my DCF-analysis estimation, they only need to grow 1/3 the rate of the overall market and stay on the current trajectory.

In the US, the connected car market is expected to grow at 11.6%, and globally, it is expected to grow at 13.4%.

There is still plenty of room to run in core markets like the US. As of 2024, only 65% of drivers were using connected technology, leaving significant expansion opportunities. Globally, the numbers look even better.

In addition to expanding penetration in current markets, Ituran also has key markets still on the table for expansion, including South Korea, Australia, and the vast majority of the Euro Zone.

According to a study by McKinsey, helping make business cases profitable and generating customer awareness and interest is the biggest unlock for expanding auto connectivity.

Specifically for customer awareness, limited hands-on experience and perceived privacy risks are the big unlock, along with a structured value proposition.

This is where Ituran can really drive growth, as they are distributing across the ecosystem and through intermediary partners. It is a lot easier to sell one finance or insurance company than one thousand individual customers. And finance and insurance companies have an easy business case, saving customers money on their loans and insurance.

Overall, Ituran is sitting in a rapidly growing industry with product and distribution channels that, in my opinion, have a perfect product-market fit. Needing to only drive 1/3 the rate of the market growth.

Downside Risk

The primary downside risk to my thesis is management execution. Ituran did have a subscriber growth stumble from 2018 to 2021.

If a situation like that were to reoccur, the price target would materially be lowered. Based on current trends and sustained growth, this is well mitigated by demonstrated management expertise, but it is still a risk investors need to consider.

Verdict

I expect Ituran to have a great report out for Q4 and full-year 2024, especially with how conservative the guidance and consensus have been as of late. This is a strong and growing business with well-managed costs and a well-diversified market and distribution strategy.

While the connected car market is expected to grow between 11 and 13% over the coming years, I have only modeled Ituran at one-third of that growth rate based on current trends and guidance. Even with only one-third of market growth, I generate a price target of $50.85, a 19% upside from today's prices.

While there are some risks if management fails to execute its continued sales and growth strategy, this is well mitigated by a 5-year run demonstrating expertise in the market.

With all of the above in mind, I rate Ituran a buy.

data.title: SolarEdge: I Was So Wrong, Sell This Rally (Double Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:SEDG) was soaring on Wednesday as investors appeared optimistic that the worst was behind the company. The company achieved positive free cash flow in the quarter and management has guided for positive free cash flow for the 2025 year. Despite having substantial losses on my long position, I am now throwing in the towel as I am now of the view that I have underestimated the competitive threats to this business. Today’s rally looks like a welcome opportunity to close my position, as I am concerned about the profitability outlook for the company. I am downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

SEDG Stock Price

I last covered SEDG in March 2024 where I rated the stock a buy due to their aggressive share repurchase program. The stock is down a staggering 65% since then, as compared to a positive 18% return from the broader market.

I don’t like having to sell at a loss — let alone a deep loss — but I was wrong here, and I don’t see reason for stubbornness. I had hoped that the negative gross margins seen in that first quarter of 2024 would prove transitory, but I was very wrong.

SEDG Stock Key Metrics

SEDG sells microinverters which are used to help maximize energy conversion across solar energy systems. A couple of years ago, investors (including yours truly) were predominantly of the view that SEDG and Enphase (ENPH) operated under a fruitful duopoly in the sector. Nowadays, Tesla (TSLA) looks like a leading disruptor, with ENPH being the other leader.

SEDG closed out 2024 with $196 million in revenue, representing a 38% YOY decline.

Unlike ENPH, which has managed to maintain solid gross margins and overall profitability, SEDG has seen its gross margins deteriorate alongside plunging sales. Gross margin came in at negative 57.2% in the quarter and negative 97.3% for the full year.

After including operating expenses, the company lost $263.7 million in operating losses in the quarter and $1.7 billion in the full year.

On the bright side, the company finally returned to positive free cash flow “generation,” with $25.5 million of free cash flow in the quarter. Management has guided for positive free cash flow for 2025. I put “generation” in quotes because with the company still being solidly unprofitable on an operational basis, this cash flow is mainly coming from other levers like inventory management. SEDG ended the quarter with $628.5 million of cash versus $676 million of debt and $432 million of warranty obligations. This company once had a solid net cash position, but the brutal solar winter has taken its toll on the balance sheet. I note that roughly half of the debt position is set to mature later this year. On the conference call, management reiterated expectations to redeem the maturing notes using cash on hand. I find this likely given that these notes carry a 0% interest rate, and it would presumably be difficult to refinance them at attractive rates, given the company’s current financial position.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the first quarter to see up to $215 million in revenue, representing a return to mid-single-digit growth and non-GAAP gross margins to come in at up to 10%. These aren’t great numbers, but they aren’t as bad as they have been over the past year, so perhaps investors are searching for reasons for optimism.

Management noted that they recorded $115 million in inventory impairments in the quarter due to weakness in the European markets. This was the second consecutive quarter with inventory impairments. Management also noted that they have revised $25.5 million of previously reported revenues and loan receivables with a struggling customer. An analyst also asked what appeared to be a potential “softball” question regarding incremental gross margins on U.S. residential inverters after excluding the “fixed COGS that have held back the margins at these lower revenue levels.” Surprisingly, management chose not to offer additional disclosure there. Quite frankly, I am surprised by the stock’s large jump today and suspect that it might have something to do with the 39% short interest.

Is SEDG Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

Consensus estimates look optimistic, as $1.2 billion in projected 2025 revenue does not look reasonable given the first quarter outlook. Yet, the stock does trade undeniably cheap at just around 1x forward sales. In comparison, ENPH trades at around 5.6x sales.

The problem is that whereas ENPH remains profitable on a GAAP basis, SEDG continues to operate at deep GAAP losses. The P/S valuation only makes sense if SEDG can eventually return to profitability, but analysts do not expect a recovery until 2026.

At this point, it is rather clear that SEDG might not be able to get out of this predicament through execution. The company has historically competed at a lower price point, and that is clearly evident in the “night and day” margin profiles as compared with ENPH. It is now looking like SEDG will need a plunge in interest rates (and associated increase in solar demand) before it can see a recovery in margins and growth rates. Even then, would it be able to recapture the market share lost to ENPH, TSLA, and others? With inflation still stubbornly persistent, I find it unreliable to bank on a fall in interest rates and believe that investors may be underestimating the risk to SEDG’s balance sheet.

SEDG Stock Conclusion

SEDG investors have been waiting for a turnaround for a long time, and the stock’s rally today seems to indicate that some believe that this has finally come. I unfortunately am skeptical and view today’s rally as an opportunity to cut losses and move on. While management is guiding for positive free cash flow this year, I continue to expect deep GAAP losses. I expect SEDG to continue to struggle in the current high interest rate environment as it loses market share to formidable competitors. I am downgrading SEDG to a sell rating.

data.title: Fiverr International Stock Selloff Is An Opportunity
data.description:
Today we are looking at the stock of Fiverr International Ltd. (NYSE:FVRR) which is selling off on its earnings report. In our opinion, this is a buying opportunity. For our followers who are unfamiliar, this is a service that connects freelancers with people who need a service or a particular skill. For example, maybe you want a logo for your new business, or perhaps you want someone to make a musical jingle for your podcast. Maybe you require someone to build a simple website. You can go to Fiverr and find people willing to do this work with up front contract pricing. It is like a giant online talent pool. So people come and connect with freelancers offering their services, and Fiverr gets a cut. That is the business model.

Over time, it has grown to have many of thousands of participants and customers on the platform, and along the way, sales have grown, as have earnings power. And they are still in growth mode, and are evolving and innovating. For example, they just launched a new AI platform for its freelancers. This will help freelancers reach new customers and protect their work, at least, that is how it is built. Further, they are offering equity to top freelancers, which could attract more content creators for growth. And on a valuation basis, this stock is really cheap. Further, the outlook for the year was above consensus. While the quarter itself was a bit of a miss, we think this selloff is a buying opportunity. Let us discuss the just reported quarter.

So the just reported quarter once again showed growth. The top line came in at $103.7 million, compared to $91.5 million a year ago. This was an increase of 13.3% year-over-year. This was a beat against consensus estimates of $2.3 million, which was quite positive. What went into this? Most of the revenue comes from the marketplace, where people are buying services. There was a 4% dip here, which may have caught some by surprise. The marketplace revenue was $73.5 million, down 4.0% compared to $76.6 last year. This was due to a smaller customer count, and this may in part be contributing to today's selling. Annual active buyers were 3.6 million at the end of 2024, 10% lower than 4.0 million at the end of 2023. But average spending of these customers is up. The average spends per buyer hit $302, compared to $278 last year, 9% higher. And, the company is taking a bigger piece of the pie, with a take rate up 20 basis points to 27.6%. We view this as quite positive overall. Where real gains are being has is in services, where revenue more than doubled to $30.2 million, versus $14.9 million last year.

Expenses are on watch here because we have seen some minor margin compression. But still, an 80% plus margin is strong. As reported, gross margins dipped 260 basis points to 80.5%. Making adjustments for one-time expenses, gross margins were 84.0%, a decrease of 60 basis points compared to a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA grew to $20.7 million, compared to $16.1 million last year, while Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 20.0% from 17.4% last year. Factoring in operating expenses, net income was $12.8 million, or $0.33 per share, compared to or $0.12 last year. That is positive. On an adjusted basis, we saw $0.64 up from $0.56 last year. However, that did miss consensus by $0.04.

From a cash flow perspective we are also seeing improvement. The net cash from operating activities grew 9% to $30.0 million, versus $27.5 million a year ago while free cash flow was up 8.1% to $29.6 million. We think it is also worth noting the entire year saw $2.38 in EPS up from $1.95 last year, which is strong growth. And for the year, free cash flow was also up 14.3% to $93.9 million.

All of this is positive news. Even if Q4 came up a little short. The reason we think this selloff is an opportunity at this valuation, at $30 per share, is the 2025 outlook was well above consensus. Initially, shares were up on the news, and are now down over 8.5% at the time of this writing, which seems punitive on a positive outlook above consensus. The consensus for Q1 revenue was $102 million, but the guide is above this even at the low end. Management sees $103.5-$108.5 million in revenue, while for the year, the guide of $422.0 to $438 million is very bullish relative to the $418.6 million consensus. On this alone it is almost a buy given the forward price to sales of 2X. But The EBITDA outlook of $82-$90 million is also positive, relative to the $74.2 million in 2024. On a per-share front, assuming comparable margins to 2024, we see EPS hitting 2.64 to $2.90. This is $2.77 at the midpoint. Giving us a really attractive 10.8X FWD valuation.

With $133 million in cash and equivalents and positive free cash flow, the debt of about $450 million long-term is very manageable. All things considered, we view this as a buying opportunity.

data.title: BNY Mellon International Equity Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
“While certain stocks have been negatively affected by the protectionist rhetoric emanating from the incoming Trump administration, as well as other geopolitical developments in Europe, we believe this can present attractive opportunities to invest in businesses where we have conviction in long-term prospects.”

Performance Summary

Weighed down by a strengthening US dollar, international equities declined over the quarter in base-currency (US dollar) terms. Digesting largely resilient economic data and the latest comments from officials on the trajectory of interest-rate cuts, the start of the review period saw investors rein in expectations that the US Federal Reserve would cut interest rates aggressively at its next policy meeting. Meanwhile, the initial enthusiasm that had welcomed China’s stimulus program waned as investors awaited greater clarity. In the UK, investors reacted to details of the Labour Party’s first Budget and the significant package of tax rises and borrowing it entailed. Elsewhere, a period of political uncertainty beckoned in Japan as Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba’s Liberal Democratic Party lost its parliamentary majority. By contrast, Donald Trump’s decisive victory in the US presidential election saw investors adopt the view that US exceptionalism, both from an economic and market perspective, would continue under a new administration. Outside of the US, however, the election received a far more cautious response. In particular, President-elect Trump's threats to impose duties on imports from Europe weighed on the region’s equity markets. Increased geopolitical uncertainty, as Russia-Ukraine tensions ramped up once again, combined with political turmoil in France, Germany and South Korea, was also unhelpful. International equities continued to weaken over the final stages of 2024. Although the US Federal Reserve trimmed interest rates in December, a more cautious outlook for rate cuts in 2025 surprised investors, rattling equity markets towards the end of the year. Fresh stimulus headlines from China were initially received positively, yet a lack of concrete detail around potential measures to boost domestic consumption disappointed once again. Meanwhile, investors continued to fret about President-elect Trump’s protectionist rhetoric.

BNY Mellon International Equity Fund outperformed its benchmark index, the MSCI EAFE (“the index”), during the fourth quarter of 2024.

The Fund fell but outperformed over the quarter, finishing the year comfortably ahead of the performance benchmark. Relative returns were boosted by strong stock selection in financials, materials and consumer discretionary. Overweight positioning in financials also proved beneficial as the incoming Trump administration presented the prospect of a looser regulatory environment in the US. On the other hand, negative stock-selection effects materialized in information technology, industrials, health care and utilities.

Top Contributors

Continuing to execute against its three-year plan laid out earlier in the year, Barclays delivered pre-tax profits above expectations for the third quarter, boosted by higher net interest income and encouraging results from the investment bank division. Apparently well-positioned to benefit from an improved fundamental and regulatory backdrop in the US under the Trump administration, Barclays’ shares continued to perform positively.

Standard Chartered (OTCPK:SCBFF)(OTCPK:SCBFY) reported pre-tax income ahead of expectations for the third quarter, boosted by a record period for its wealth business. The bank raised its capital distribution outlook, revenue guidance and return on tangible equity target, a key measure of profitability. Management is taking action to simplify the business, which we believe should help to generate high-quality growth and improve profitability over the medium term.

Heidelberg Materials (OTCPK:HDLMY) performed strongly following robust results for the third quarter as sales and earnings registered above consensus. The announcement of a new self-help plan, named ‘Transformation Accelerator’, was received positively by investors.

Shares in Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF) declined as its next-generation obesity drug CagriSema disappointed in a late-stage trial. Consequently, the fund’s underweight positioning in this sizeable index constituent proved beneficial.

Top Detractors

Andritz (OTCPK:ADRZF)(OTCPK:ADRZY) reported a mixed set of quarterly results as sales underwhelmed. Downbeat management commentary around the current demand environment for the company’s end markets called into question the prospects of a near-term recovery.

UK housebuilder British Land (OTCPK:BTLCY)(OTCPK:BRLAF) retreated as investors reacted to the possibility of a harsher rate environment following the government’s Budget announcement.

Pressured by rising bund yields and political uncertainty in Germany, shares in utility business E.ON (OTCPK:EONGY)(OTCPK:ENAKF) declined over the quarter. However, we continue to like company fundamentals despite the short-term vagaries of the political backdrop.

Amid continued concern around its competitiveness in high-bandwidth memory chips, Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) struggled. Although news of a share buyback was welcomed by investors during the quarter, a reshuffling of management in its chip division saw a muted market response. Renewed US efforts to prevent China from advancing its chipmaking capabilities also weighed.

Market Outlook

Investor worries around the US labor market have ebbed in recent months, while concerns about stalling progress in the fight to bring inflation down have returned. Indeed, a key area of focus is likely to remain the outlook for interest-rate cuts in 2025. Expectations have grown increasingly cautious of late, particularly given the potential inflationary impact of President-elect Trump’s apparent desire to impose tariffs, implement tax cuts and reduce regulation. As markets consider the pace of interest-rate cuts and the rhetoric around any given move, labor markets, inflation and growth numbers look set to remain front of mind. Meanwhile, market participants have seemingly adopted the view that a period of US exceptionalism, both from an economic and market perspective, is likely to continue under a Trump administration. US equity market valuations are higher than those in other regions, and while the outperformance of US stocks may continue, its recent magnitude could prove hard to match in 2025. The positive performance of technology companies has dominated the market, yet there have been signs over 2024 that the artificial intelligence (AI) theme is coming under closer scrutiny. While certain stocks have been negatively affected by the protectionist rhetoric emanating from the incoming Trump administration, as well as other geopolitical developments in Europe, we believe this can present attractive opportunities to invest in businesses where we have conviction in long-term prospects.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Diversification cannot assure a profit or protect against loss. Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks, to varying degrees. Currencies are subject to the risk that those currencies will decline in value relative to a local currency, or, in the case of hedged positions, that the local currency will decline relative to the currency being hedged. Each of these risks could increase the fund's volatility. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries.

Index Definitions

The Morgan Stanley Capital International Europe, Australasia, Far East (MSCI EAFE®) Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure equity performance in developed markets excluding the United States and Canada. The index consists of select designated MSCI national developed market indices. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Definitions

Q is quarter. NAV is Net Asset Value. YTD is Year to Date. FDIC is Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. Tangible equity target, or tangible common equity (TCE), is calculated by subtracting intangible assets (including goodwill) and preferred equity from the company's book value.
